title: Add scene context metadata (POV, location, timeframe) with filterable navigator
description:
## Problem
Writers lose narrative orientation when drafting across many scenes because key context (POV, location, timeframe) is not consistently visible or searchable. This increases continuity mistakes and slows revision.

## Target user
Multi-chapter fiction writers who juggle multiple POV characters, locations, and timelines while drafting and revising.

## Outcome / KPI
- Reduce continuity-related scene revisions per manuscript
- Increase weekly active use of scene navigator filters
- Decrease average time to find target scene during revision

## Scope
- Add scene-level metadata fields: `POV character`, `location`, `timeframe` (free text v1 with optional suggestions from existing worldbuilding entities)
- Surface metadata in scene cards/navigator rows and at top of scene editor
- Add filter controls in chapter/scene navigator for POV, location, and timeframe
- Keep metadata editable from both scene settings and scene card quick-edit
- Persist metadata for use in existing timeline/continuity workflows

## Non-goals
- Automatic contradiction detection rules
- Complex ontology management for tags/entities
- AI-generated scene metadata

## Acceptance criteria
- User can set/edit POV, location, and timeframe on any scene
- Scene navigator can filter by each metadata field independently and in combination
- Active filters are clearly visible and can be reset in one action
- Metadata appears in the scene editor header without obstructing writing flow
- Metadata values persist across reload and are available in timeline views

## Priority rationale
This builds a practical writing-flow advantage aligned with Living Writer/Campfire inspiration: keep structure context visible while drafting, not hidden in separate planning screens. It also compounds value of in-flight navigator and timeline features already on the roadmap.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

<plan>

## Goal
Add scene-level context metadata (POV character, location, timeframe) with visible in-editor display and filterable corkboard navigation.

## Scope and files

**Modified files (6):**
1. `src/types/scene.ts` - Add optional metadata fields: `pov?`, `location?`, `timeframe?`
2. `src/lib/scenes.ts` - Add `updateSceneMetadata` helper function
3. `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` - Add metadata row to editor, wire up metadata updates
4. `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` - Metadata row styles
5. `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx` - Add filter state and filtering logic
6. `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx` - Add metadata display line

**New files (4):**
1. `src/components/MetadataRow/MetadataRow.tsx` - Metadata input fields for editor
2. `src/components/MetadataRow/MetadataRow.module.css` - Metadata row styles
3. `src/components/MetadataFilters/MetadataFilters.tsx` - Filter controls for corkboard
4. `src/components/MetadataFilters/MetadataFilters.module.css` - Filter styles

## Step-by-step plan

### Phase 1: Data model extension

**Step 1.1: Add metadata fields to Scene type**
- File: `src/types/scene.ts`
- Add optional fields: `pov?: string`, `location?: string`, `timeframe?: string`
- All optional to support existing scenes without metadata

**Step 1.2: Add metadata update helper**
- File: `src/lib/scenes.ts`
- Add function: `updateSceneMetadata(storyId: string, sceneId: string, metadata: { pov?: string; location?: string; timeframe?: string }): void`
- Fetches scene, updates metadata fields, persists to localStorage
- Preserves existing fields (title, summary, status, order)

### Phase 2: Editor metadata row

**Step 2.1: Build MetadataRow component**
- Files: `src/components/MetadataRow/MetadataRow.tsx`, `.module.css`
- Props: `pov?: string`, `location?: string`, `timeframe?: string`, `onChange: (field: string, value: string) => void`, `readOnly?: boolean`
- Render: Three inline input groups with labels "POV", "Location", "Timeframe"
- Inputs: Text inputs with placeholder "Add POV" when empty
- Keyboard: Tab order left-to-right, all inputs accessible
- Style: Compact horizontal row, labels always visible (`--font-size-sm`, `--color-elephant-500`), inputs use existing form input styles
- Accessibility: Explicit labels, not placeholder-only

**Step 2.2: Integrate metadata row into editor**
- File: `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`
- In `editorHeader`, add `<MetadataRow>` below title input, above editor canvas
- Pass current scene metadata as props
- Wire `onChange` to call `updateSceneMetadata` with debouncing (500ms, same as content auto-save)
- Handle null/undefined selectedScene gracefully (hide metadata row if no scene selected)

**Step 2.3: Style metadata row in editor layout**
- File: `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css`
- Add `.metadataRow` class: horizontal layout, spacing between fields
- Use existing token values for padding, borders, colors
- Maintain visual rhythm with existing editor header

### Phase 3: Corkboard filtering

**Step 3.1: Build MetadataFilters component**
- Files: `src/components/MetadataFilters/MetadataFilters.tsx`, `.module.css`
- Props: `povOptions: string[]`, `locationOptions: string[]`, `timeframeOptions: string[]`, `activePov: string | null`, `activeLocation: string | null`, `activeTimeframe: string | null`, `onFilterChange: (field: string, value: string | null) => void`, `onClearAll: () => void`
- Render: Three filter dropdowns/selects labeled "POV", "Location", "Timeframe", plus "Clear filters" button
- Dropdowns: "All" default option, then sorted unique values from scenes
- Clear all button: Visible when any filter active, resets all to null
- Style: Horizontal row, compact controls, grayscale tokens only
- Accessibility: Labels, keyboard-navigable dropdowns

**Step 3.2: Add filter state to Corkboard**
- File: `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx`
- Add state: `const [activePov, setActivePov] = useState<string | null>(null)` (same for location, timeframe)
- Derive unique metadata values from scenes array: `const povOptions = Array.from(new Set(scenes.map(s => s.pov).filter(Boolean)))`
- Filter scenes array before rendering: scenes that match all active filters (AND logic)
- Display filtered count: "Showing X of Y scenes" when filters active
- Empty state: "No scenes match filters" with "Clear filters" action

**Step 3.3: Integrate filters into corkboard toolbar**
- File: `src/components/Corkboard/Corkboard.tsx`
- In toolbar (currently has `<ViewModeSwitch>`), add `<MetadataFilters>` in `toolbarRight` area per Design brief
- Pass filter state and callbacks
- Ensure toolbar remains single row on desktop, wraps gracefully on tablet/mobile

### Phase 4: Metadata display in scene cards

**Step 4.1: Add metadata line to SceneCard**
- Files: `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx`, `.module.css`
- Between `summary` and `footer`, add new `.metadata` section
- Render: Three compact chips/labels: "POV: {value}", "Loc: {location}", "Time: {timeframe}"
- Hide chip if value is empty/undefined
- Truncate long values with ellipsis, show full value in title attribute (tooltip)
- Style: Small font (`--font-size-xs`), muted color (`--color-cement-400`), horizontal layout with spacing

**Step 4.2: Add quick-edit affordance (optional for v1)**
- File: `src/components/SceneCard/SceneCard.tsx`
- Add "Edit context" link/button in footer (next to status select)
- On click: Open inline popover or inline edit mode with three text inputs
- On save: Call `onMetadataChange` callback (added to props), which calls `updateSceneMetadata`
- Accessibility: Keyboard-accessible, focus management
- Note: If time-constrained, defer quick-edit to v2; users can edit in editor mode first

### Phase 5: Empty states and polish

**Step 5.1: Handle missing metadata gracefully**
- MetadataRow: Show placeholders "Add POV" when empty, not blank inputs
- SceneCard: Omit metadata chips entirely if all three fields empty (not "POV: —")
- Corkboard: If no scenes have metadata yet, hide filter controls or show muted empty state

**Step 5.2: Filter persistence (optional)**
- Store active filters in sessionStorage with key `story-${storyId}-filters`
- Restore on corkboard mount
- Clear on unmount or when switching stories
- Note: sessionStorage, not localStorage, to avoid sticky filters across sessions

**Step 5.3: Success feedback for metadata saves**
- Optional: Show subtle "Saved" indicator near metadata row for 1-2 seconds after save
- Keep low-friction, non-blocking (not toast/alert)
- Use existing auto-save pattern from editor if implemented

### Phase 6: Responsive behavior

**Step 6.1: Metadata row responsive layout**
- File: `src/components/MetadataRow/MetadataRow.module.css`
- Desktop: Three fields in one horizontal row, equal width
- Tablet (768-1023px): Three fields may reduce width but stay horizontal
- Mobile (< 768px): Stack vertically, full-width inputs

**Step 6.2: Filter controls responsive layout**
- File: `src/components/MetadataFilters/MetadataFilters.module.css`
- Desktop: Horizontal row in toolbar right area
- Tablet: May wrap to second row, stays above grid
- Mobile: Compact horizontal row with smaller dropdowns, or collapse to single "Filter" button with modal/drawer

## Test plan

**Unit tests (if framework exists):**
- `src/lib/scenes.ts`: Test `updateSceneMetadata` updates specific fields without affecting others
- Metadata extraction: Test deriving unique POV/location/timeframe values from scene arrays
- Filter logic: Test AND combination (scene with POV=A, Location=B passes filter {POV: A, Location: B})

**Integration tests:**
1. Add metadata to scene in editor → Verify persists to localStorage
2. Reload page → Verify metadata displays in editor and scene card
3. Set POV filter in corkboard → Verify only scenes with that POV visible
4. Set multiple filters (POV + Location) → Verify only scenes matching both criteria visible
5. Clear filters → Verify all scenes return
6. Create new scene → Verify starts with empty metadata (no crash)

**Manual testing:**

- **Keyboard accessibility:**
  - Tab through metadata inputs in logical order (POV → Location → Timeframe)
  - Tab through filter dropdowns
  - Keyboard-accessible "Clear filters" button

- **Visual clarity:**
  - Labels always visible (not just placeholders)
  - Metadata chips in cards truncate long values without breaking layout
  - Filter controls clearly show active state

- **Responsive:**
  - Desktop: Metadata row horizontal, filter controls in toolbar right
  - Tablet: Metadata row stays horizontal, filters may wrap
  - Mobile: Metadata row stacks vertically, filter controls compact or drawer

- **Data integrity:**
  - Scenes without metadata don't crash or show "undefined"
  - Updating metadata preserves title, summary, status, order
  - Filter state doesn't persist inappropriately across sessions

- **Edge cases:**
  - Very long POV/location/timeframe values (50+ chars): Truncate with ellipsis, tooltip shows full value
  - Special characters in metadata (quotes, emojis): Display correctly, no encoding issues
  - All scenes lack metadata: Filter controls hidden or show empty state
  - No scenes match active filters: Show clear empty state with "Clear filters" action

**Commands:**
```bash
npm run dev
# Visit http://localhost:3000/story/test-1
# Test metadata input in editor
# Switch to corkboard, test filters
# Check localStorage: story-test-1-scenes should include metadata fields
```

## Risks and mitigations

**Risk 1: Metadata fields too open-ended**
- Problem: Users enter inconsistent values ("POV: Alice" vs "Alice" vs "alice")
- Impact: Filters less useful, many options in dropdown
- Mitigation v1: Accept free text, show sorted unique values. Future: Add autocomplete from existing values or worldbuilding entities.

**Risk 2: Filter UX complexity**
- Problem: AND logic with three filters may be confusing (empty results when too restrictive)
- Impact: Users frustrated by "No scenes" message
- Mitigation: Show filtered count "X of Y scenes", make "Clear filters" prominent. Future: Add OR toggle or filter by "any of" logic.

**Risk 3: Performance with many unique metadata values**
- Problem: 50+ unique POV characters = large dropdown, slow rendering
- Impact: Laggy filter controls
- Mitigation: Limit dropdown to most recent 20 values, add search input if needed. Use memoization for unique value extraction.

**Risk 4: Metadata row clutters editor**
- Problem: Three input fields take vertical space, reduce canvas area
- Impact: Writers feel cramped
- Mitigation: Keep row compact (single line), use small font, consider collapsible metadata section in future if feedback is negative.

**Risk 5: Quick-edit in scene card adds complexity**
- Problem: Inline edit mode in card is non-trivial UI work
- Impact: Schedule risk for v1 delivery
- Mitigation: Defer quick-edit to v2; users can edit metadata in editor mode for v1. Card displays read-only metadata.

## Rollback strategy

**If critical bugs found:**
1. Revert PR merge commit
2. Existing scenes without metadata continue working (optional fields)
3. No data loss: Metadata fields added, never removed from existing scenes

**Graceful degradation:**
- If metadata undefined: Treat as empty string, show placeholder
- If filter logic fails: Show all scenes unfiltered (fail open, not fail closed)
- If MetadataRow crashes: Wrap in error boundary, show "Metadata unavailable" message

## Observability

**Logs:**
- `src/lib/scenes.ts`: Console log on `updateSceneMetadata` → "Updated metadata for scene {id}: {pov}, {location}, {timeframe}"
- Corkboard: Log filter changes → "Applied filters: POV={value}, Location={value}, Timeframe={value}"
- Log filtered scene count → "Showing {filtered} of {total} scenes"

**Metrics (future):**
- Track metadata field completion rate (% of scenes with POV, location, timeframe)
- Track filter usage frequency (how often users apply filters)
- Track average unique values per metadata field (helps inform autocomplete UX)

**Traces:**
- Not applicable for localStorage v1

## Acceptance mapping

| Acceptance Criteria | Implementation |
|---------------------|----------------|
| User can set/edit POV, location, and timeframe on any scene | MetadataRow in editor with three text inputs → updateSceneMetadata saves to localStorage |
| Scene navigator can filter by each metadata field independently and in combination | MetadataFilters in corkboard toolbar with three dropdowns → filter logic (AND) → filtered scenes array |
| Active filters are clearly visible and can be reset in one action | Filter dropdowns show selected values, "Clear filters" button visible when filters active → resets all to null |
| Metadata appears in the scene editor header without obstructing writing flow | MetadataRow placed below title, above canvas, compact horizontal layout, grayscale tokens |
| Metadata values persist across reload and are available in timeline views | localStorage persistence via updateSceneMetadata → getScenes loads metadata → scene cards display metadata |

## Handoff

**Implementation readiness:**
- ✅ Product requirements reviewed
- ✅ Design brief reviewed
- ✅ Current Scene type and Corkboard/SceneCard architecture understood
- ✅ Implementation plan complete with 6 phases

**Code Monkey: Key implementation notes**
1. **Phase order:** Data model → Editor → Filters → Card display → Polish → Responsive
2. **Optional fields:** All metadata fields are optional, support existing scenes without metadata
3. **Filter logic:** AND combination (scene must match all active filters to be visible)
4. **Quick-edit in cards:** Optional for v1, can defer to v2 if time-constrained
5. **Grayscale tokens only:** Reference `src/styles/tokens.css` for all colors
6. **Refer to Design brief** for exact field order (POV, Location, Timeframe) and visual hierarchy

Assign to: Code Monkey



</plan>

title: Add anchored beta-reader comments on shared chapter previews
description:
## Problem
Writers need targeted chapter feedback from trusted readers, but generic sharing lacks structured comments tied to specific text or scene context. Without in-context feedback, revision cycles are slow and noisy.

## Target user
Writers sharing draft chapters privately with beta readers for pre-publication revision input.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase number of shared previews that receive actionable feedback
- Increase chapter revision completion rate after feedback is received
- Reduce time from share to first accepted revision

## Scope
- Extend read-only chapter preview links with optional inline comment mode for invited readers
- Let readers leave anchored comments on paragraph/scene blocks plus general chapter notes
- Add writer inbox for incoming feedback with filters (open, resolved, by chapter)
- Allow writer to resolve comments and mark planned follow-up changes
- Track basic feedback metadata (reader alias, timestamp, chapter reference)

## Non-goals
- Public community feed/discovery mechanics
- Full real-time co-author editing
- Native mobile push notifications

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can enable/disable comments per shared preview link
- Reader can submit anchored comments without edit permission to manuscript text
- Writer can view, resolve, and reopen comments from a dedicated feedback panel
- Resolved/open state persists across reload and is reflected in chapter view
- Comment links preserve manuscript privacy and respect share-link permissions

## Priority rationale
This builds directly on shareable previews and introduces an Inkitt-inspired feedback loop while keeping the product private-draft-first. It accelerates revision quality without requiring public publishing features.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Add manuscript import wizard for .docx/.txt with chapter-scene detection
description:
## Problem
Many target users already draft in Google Docs, Word, or plain text. Without import, switching costs are high and onboarding stalls before users experience the app’s structure/planning advantages.

## Target user
Writers migrating an in-progress manuscript from general-purpose editors into a story-focused workspace.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase activation rate from signup to first meaningful edit session
- Reduce drop-off during first-time story creation
- Increase imported manuscripts that reach at least one saved revision inside app

## Scope
- Add an import entry point from Home/Stories (`Import manuscript`)
- Support `.docx` and `.txt` uploads up to a defined size limit
- Parse headings into chapter candidates with editable mapping before final import
- Create scenes from detected chapter sections with fallback to single-scene import
- Show import summary: chapters/scenes created, unresolved formatting warnings

## Non-goals
- Perfect formatting parity for every Word style
- PDF import
- One-click cloud provider sync integrations

## Acceptance criteria
- User can upload `.docx` or `.txt` and complete import without leaving app
- User can review/edit chapter mapping before finalizing import
- Imported manuscript opens in chapter/scene navigator with preserved text content
- Unsupported formatting is surfaced as non-blocking warnings, not silent data loss
- Failed imports provide clear, actionable error message and retry path

## Priority rationale
Import removes a major adoption barrier and accelerates progress toward the company goal by letting real writers bring real work into the app immediately.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Add universal command palette for instant manuscript navigation and actions
description:
## Problem
As manuscripts grow, writers lose momentum when they must hunt through sidebar hierarchies to find scenes, chapters, notes, or common actions. Navigation friction breaks drafting flow and increases context-switch cost.

## Target user
Writers actively drafting or revising medium-to-long manuscripts (20+ scenes) who need fast keyboard-first movement.

## Outcome / KPI
- Reduce median time-to-open-target-scene during revision sessions
- Increase weekly usage of keyboard navigation workflows
- Improve session depth (more words/edits per session after feature adoption)

## Scope
- Add a command palette opened by keyboard shortcut (e.g., Cmd/Ctrl+K)
- Support fuzzy search across stories, chapters, scenes, and worldbuilding entities
- Include quick actions: create scene, rename scene, jump to timeline/corkboard, open story settings
- Show recent items and last-opened scenes for one-step resume
- Preserve current view mode when possible when jumping between scenes

## Non-goals
- AI-generated commands or natural-language intent parsing
- Replacing existing sidebar or navigator UI
- Full-text semantic search over manuscript body in v1

## Acceptance criteria
- User can open/close palette entirely with keyboard
- Search results include at least stories, chapters, scenes, and worldbuilding entities
- Selecting a result navigates to the correct destination in one action
- Quick actions perform successfully and provide immediate feedback
- Palette remains responsive with 200+ scenes in local test data

## Priority rationale
Now that navigator, corkboard, timeline, and worldbuilding surfaces are expanding, retrieval speed becomes a core UX bottleneck. This feature directly supports Living Writer-style clarity while giving a differentiated speed advantage for power users.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Link worldbuilding entities inline in the editor with mention chips and quick peek
description:
## Problem
Writers track characters, places, and lore in separate panels, but references inside prose are not structurally linked. This creates continuity drift and forces manual cross-checking during revision.

## Target user
Fiction writers managing recurring characters/locations/rules who need context while drafting without leaving the editor.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase usage of linked worldbuilding entities per manuscript
- Reduce continuity-related revisions tied to character/location inconsistency
- Increase frequency of worldbuilding panel interactions during drafting sessions

## Scope
- Add inline mention trigger in editor (e.g., @) to insert linked entity chips for character/location/rule items
- Store and render mentions as durable links to worldbuilding records
- Add hover/click quick peek card showing key entity details without full panel switch
- Provide mention management in scene metadata or inspector (list all mentions in current scene)
- Keep plain-text export fallback by converting mention chips to readable text labels

## Non-goals
- Automatic NER/entity detection from raw prose
- Complex bi-directional graph analytics in v1
- Multi-user mention resolution workflows

## Acceptance criteria
- User can insert entity mentions from the editor via keyboard and mouse
- Mention chips render consistently after reload and scene switches
- Clicking/hovering a mention opens a lightweight entity peek with core fields
- Removing an entity from worldbuilding does not corrupt manuscript text
- Export output remains readable when mentions are present

## Priority rationale
Campfire demonstrates demand for strong world context, but often in separate modules. Inline linking keeps the writer in flow and can become a differentiation point: context-rich drafting without UI sprawl.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Review and merge: BIN-64
description:
## Summary

Code review complete for BIN-64 (corkboard scene planning feature). All critical and major issues have been resolved in revision commit.

**Feature:** Add corkboard-style scene cards for story planning

**Branch:** `ticket/BIN-64`

**Status:** ✅ Approved by Code Reviewer

title: Add read-aloud revision mode with pacing, repetition, and dialogue clarity highlights
description:
## Problem
Many narrative issues (awkward cadence, repeated phrasing, unnatural dialogue beats) are hard to catch while silently reading. Writers need a revision pass tuned for voice and flow quality.

## Target user
Drafting-stage novelists and editors polishing prose quality before beta-reader sharing or submission.

## Outcome / KPI
- 25% increase in usage of revision tooling per active manuscript.
- 20% decrease in repeat phrasing flags in final manuscript versions.
- 15% faster time from first draft to “ready for feedback” milestone.

## Scope
- Add a read-aloud mode at chapter/scene level with play/pause and sentence-follow cursor.
- Highlight potential prose issues during playback: repeated phrases, long-run sentences, and dense dialogue blocks lacking beats/tags.
- Provide quick actions from each highlight: jump to sentence, dismiss, or mark for later.
- Add “Revision pass summary” with counts by issue type and completion progress.
- Preserve writer control: all suggestions are optional and reversible.

## Non-goals
- Fully autonomous rewriting of scenes.
- Language translation or multilingual grammar tooling.
- Accessibility-grade screen reader replacement.

## Acceptance criteria
- User can run read-aloud mode on any scene/chapter and see synchronized sentence focus.
- System surfaces at least three issue classes (repetition, long sentence cadence, dialogue clarity) with clickable locations.
- Dismissing an issue removes it from active list without editing text automatically.
- Revision summary updates after edits and supports rerun.
- Feature remains usable without enabling broader AI generation features.

## Priority rationale
Living Writer-style structure helps writers organize stories; this feature improves prose finish quality directly in-app, creating a stronger “draft to publish-ready” workflow and a differentiated quality pass beyond basic outlining.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Create submission tracker for query rounds, agent replies, and revision follow-ups
description:
## Problem
After manuscript completion, writers lose context across submissions (who was queried, when responses arrived, what changes were requested). Spreadsheets and inbox threads break continuity and reduce follow-through.

## Target user
Writers preparing to query agents, submit to contests, or run structured beta-reader rounds.

## Outcome / KPI
- 35% of users who export a submission package also create at least one tracked submission entry.
- 20% increase in completed follow-up actions within target response windows.
- Reduction in missed response deadlines and duplicate submissions.

## Scope
- Add submission tracker workspace tied to a manuscript.
- Track entries with status, recipient, sent date, response date, response type, and next action date.
- Provide timeline and board views (e.g., Drafting, Submitted, Requested Revisions, Closed).
- Add reminder nudges for overdue follow-ups and pending requested revisions.
- Link each submission entry to exported package version or manuscript snapshot used at send time.

## Non-goals
- Automated email sending from the app.
- Marketplace/discovery functionality for publishers or agents.
- Contract/legal workflow management.

## Acceptance criteria
- User can create, edit, and archive submission entries per manuscript.
- Board/timeline views accurately reflect submission status transitions.
- Reminder state appears for overdue follow-up dates.
- Entry can reference a specific export/snapshot artifact so writer knows exactly what was sent.
- Data persists and is included in manuscript-level backups.

## Priority rationale
This extends the product from drafting into real publishing workflow outcomes, reducing drop-off after completion. It complements the existing package builder while creating a practical end-to-end moat for serious writers.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Create submission package builder for query letter, synopses, and beta-reader packet
description:
## Problem
Writers can draft manuscripts but still face major friction when preparing submission deliverables. They must manually assemble query letters, synopses, and beta-reader materials in separate tools, causing inconsistency and duplicated effort.

## Target user
Writers approaching submission-readiness who need a repeatable handoff workflow from manuscript to external deliverables.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase percentage of completed manuscripts that generate at least one submission package
- Reduce time from draft-complete to first external submission/share event
- Improve perceived end-to-end workflow satisfaction

## Scope
- Add `Submission Package` workspace at story level
- Generate editable templates for: query letter, one-page synopsis, long synopsis, and beta-reader briefing sheet
- Auto-link core manuscript metadata (title, genre, logline, comps, protagonist/conflict placeholders) into templates
- Include versioning for package drafts with timestamps and status labels (draft, ready, submitted)
- Export package as DOCX bundle and plain-text copy blocks

## Non-goals
- Agent/discovery marketplace integrations
- Automated email sending or CRM workflow
- Legal contract generation

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can create a submission package from an existing story in under 5 minutes
- All package documents are editable and save independently
- Metadata changes can be refreshed into templates with explicit user confirmation
- Writer can export package artifacts without losing template structure
- Package status and version history persist per story

## Priority rationale
Market Research identified publishing handoff as a concrete gap where competitors are weaker. This feature expands the app from drafting tool to full manuscript workflow, improving adoption and retention near the finish line.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

## Decision
approve

## Differentiation thesis
This moves us from "drafting tool" to "submission-ready workflow". Competitors emphasize writing structure, worldbuilding, or reader community; a fast, integrated submission package builder is a concrete endgame advantage for writers preparing outreach.

## Competitor evidence
- LivingWriter’s pricing/features highlight plotting and export capabilities (e.g., Advanced Plot Board, export formats), but not a guided submission-package workflow (query + synopsis + beta packet): https://livingwriter.com/pricing
- Campfire’s feature positioning emphasizes modules for planning/worldbuilding (story outlines, interactive maps, worldbuilding tools), not manuscript-to-submission packaging: https://www.campfirewriting.com/interactive-maps
- Inkitt’s writer guidance centers on reader growth/community engagement and platform presence, indicating a different value focus than submission prep tooling: https://www.inkitt.com/writersblog/inkitt-author-/

## Risks / trade-offs
- Scope creep risk if this expands into CRM/emailing workflows.
- Template quality must balance flexibility with enough guidance for novice submitters.
- Export fidelity (DOCX + plain text) can become a support burden if formatting is inconsistent.

## Recommendation
Approve with a design brief that prioritizes speed-to-first-package (<5 minutes), low-friction metadata refresh UX, and clear status/version semantics so submission prep feels reliable rather than heavyweight.

[Design]:
## UX goal
Enable a writer to create, edit, and export a complete submission package in under 5 minutes from the story workspace, with a flow that feels as direct as drafting.

## Information hierarchy
1. Story context: package title, current status (`draft`, `ready`, `submitted`), last updated timestamp.
2. Core actions: `Create package`, `Refresh metadata`, `Export DOCX bundle`, `Copy plain text`.
3. Document workspace: Query Letter, One-Page Synopsis, Long Synopsis, Beta-Reader Briefing as first-class editable tabs/cards.
4. Version history: chronological list of saved versions with status badges and restore/view affordance.
5. Supporting metadata panel: title, genre, logline, comps, protagonist/conflict placeholders with clear source-of-truth messaging.

## Layout and components
- Add a story-level mode switch option for `Submission Package` alongside existing `Editor`/`Corkboard` patterns (same one-click interaction model).
- Use a two-column desktop layout aligned to current story page composition:
  - Main column: selected document editor with template scaffold and inline section headings.
  - Right rail: package status card, metadata snapshot, version history list, export actions.
- On first entry, show a focused “Create submission package” setup panel (single CTA + lightweight checklist).
- Document navigation should use a compact segmented/tab row (4 document types) anchored above the editor body.
- Reuse existing card/button/input visual language from stories and story page headers; avoid introducing novel control styles.

## Interaction states
- Idle:
  - Existing package shows current document content immediately and active document tab.
  - `Refresh metadata` is available with a subtle “last synced” label.
- Loading:
  - Skeleton rows for version list and placeholder blocks in editor body.
  - Export buttons show disabled loading text (`Preparing export…`).
- Empty:
  - No package yet: single callout card with 3-step explanation (generate, edit, export) and one primary CTA.
  - Empty version history: helper copy “Versions appear after first save.”
- Error:
  - Inline banner in main column (non-modal) with plain-language failure and retry action.
  - Export failures should preserve current edits and show retry without page reset.
- Success:
  - Non-blocking confirmation toast/inline notice after save, metadata refresh, and export.
  - Version creation confirms timestamp + status badge added to history.

## Accessibility
- Keyboard:
  - Full tab order: mode switch -> document tabs -> editor -> metadata actions -> version list.
  - Arrow key support for document tablist; Enter/Space activates actions.
- Focus:
  - Reuse current high-contrast focus outlines (`--color-carbon-600`) on all interactive controls.
  - Do not trap focus except in explicit confirmation dialog for metadata refresh.
- Contrast:
  - Keep text/background combinations at AA minimum using existing grayscale tokens.
  - Status badges need text contrast distinct from surrounding card backgrounds.
- Text clarity:
  - Use direct writer-facing labels (“Query letter”, “Long synopsis”) and avoid system jargon.

## Visual style
- Follow existing grayscale system in `src/styles/tokens.css`; use only approved tokens (`--color-black-700` through `--color-polar-100`, `--color-white`).
- Preserve current calm, document-first tone: clean surfaces, subtle borders, no saturated accents.
- Typography should match app hierarchy:
  - Page/document headers: semibold sans token scale.
  - Body writing fields: serif editor treatment consistent with current story editor.
- Use status chips with grayscale differentiation (border + fill + label) rather than color-only signaling.

## Responsive behavior
- Desktop (>=1024px): two-column editing workspace (main editor + right rail) with sticky top action strip.
- Tablet (768-1023px): collapse right rail below editor as stacked cards; keep document tabs fixed at top of content.
- Mobile (<768px): single-column flow; tabs become horizontally scrollable pills; actions prioritize `Save` and `Export` with secondary options under overflow.
- Maintain one-tap mode switching and avoid deep nesting/modals on mobile.

## Codebase alignment
- Reviewed architecture and primitives:
  - `src/app/layout.tsx`
  - `src/components/AppShell/AppShell.tsx`
  - `src/components/Navigation/Navigation.tsx`
  - `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx` + `page.module.css`
  - `src/components/ViewModeSwitch/ViewModeSwitch.tsx`
  - `src/styles/tokens.css`
- Recommendation is intentionally aligned to current story-route-centered UX and CSS-module token usage.

## Reuse vs new build
- Reuse:
  - `ViewModeSwitch` interaction model for one-click mode changes.
  - Existing header/title input patterns from story page.
  - Existing card, button, and focus-outline patterns from Stories/Home surfaces.
- New build:
  - Submission Package document tab component (4 docs).
  - Version history list component with status chip.
  - Metadata refresh confirmation flow specific to template overwrite behavior.
  - Export action group for DOCX bundle + plain-text copy blocks.

## Feasibility notes
- Metadata refresh must be explicit and diff-aware in UX: user confirms before overwriting edited template regions.
- Independent per-document saves require clear per-document dirty/saved indicators to prevent cross-document ambiguity.
- Version history semantics should be package-level snapshots with traceable timestamp/status; avoid hidden autosave-only behavior.
- Export UX should tolerate long-running generation without blocking active editing.

## Constraints / implementation notes
- Add the new mode entry point where story modes are controlled today: `src/components/ViewModeSwitch/ViewModeSwitch.tsx` and integration in `src/app/story/[id]/page.tsx`.
- Keep page-level structure consistent with current story editor containers in `src/app/story/[id]/page.module.css` (header band, main canvas, optional side rail behavior).
- Follow layout shell and spacing constraints from `src/components/AppShell/AppShell.module.css` and `src/components/ContentContainer/ContentContainer.module.css`.
- Use only existing token primitives from `src/styles/tokens.css` for color, spacing, typography, borders, focus, and elevation.

## Handoff

title: Create lightweight timeline view for chronological consistency
description:
## Problem
Writers struggle to track chronology across scenes, causing continuity errors and rework late in the drafting process.

## Target user
Novelists managing multi-thread stories with time-sensitive events.

## Outcome / KPI
- Reduce reported continuity-conflict edits during revision.
- Increase number of stories using timeline metadata per scene.

## Scope
- Add optional timeline metadata to scenes (date/time or relative order).
- Provide a timeline view listing scenes in chronological order independent of manuscript order.
- Flag obvious chronology conflicts (e.g., duplicate impossible ordering within same storyline tag).
- Enable click-through from timeline entry to scene editor.

## Non-goals
- Full calendar app integration.
- Complex simulation of travel/logistics constraints.
- AI auto-fixing timeline conflicts.

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can set and edit timeline metadata for a scene.
- Timeline view renders scenes by chronological order and supports navigation to scene drafts.
- At least one deterministic chronology conflict rule is enforced and surfaced to the writer.
- Timeline metadata persists and survives refresh/reload.

## Priority rationale
Timeline support improves structural quality and reduces revision pain, making the app more credible for long-form fiction projects.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Add worldbuilding panel with linked story elements
description:
## Problem
Writers need fast access to character/location/lore context while drafting, but context switching to separate notes breaks flow.

## Target user
Writers building rich fictional worlds with recurring entities and rules.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase count of stories using reusable worldbuilding entities.
- Reduce context-switching events away from active drafting views.

## Scope
- Add worldbuilding entities: character, location, and lore item (minimal schema).
- Add a side panel in editor to browse/search entities and pin relevant items per scene.
- Enable linking entities to scenes and viewing linked entities inline in scene context.
- Keep panel collapsible to preserve distraction-free writing space.

## Non-goals
- Advanced map builders, language generators, or magic-system simulation.
- Public wiki publishing for story worlds.
- Real-time collaborative world editing.

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can create/edit/delete character, location, and lore entities.
- Writer can link/unlink entities to a scene from the editor.
- Scene view shows linked entities without leaving the draft context.
- Panel can be collapsed and reopened without losing draft state.

## Priority rationale
This captures a key Campfire-inspired advantage while keeping the writing surface clean, supporting differentiation beyond a plain document editor.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Create outline corkboard linked to scenes for planning and reordering
description:
## Problem
Writers need to plan story beats before and during drafting, but the app has no practical planning surface. Without an outline/corkboard, users bounce to external tools and lose context.

## Target user
Planner and hybrid writers who map scenes before drafting and frequently adjust sequence during revisions.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase percentage of active manuscripts with at least one outline card
- Increase scene reorder actions completed inside app (vs external tools)
- Improve week-2 retention for planner/hybrid writing styles

## Scope
- Add Outline view with card-based scene summaries (one card per scene)
- Each card includes: scene title, intent, POV, and optional status tag
- Reordering cards updates the underlying scene order
- Clicking a card jumps user to that scene in editor
- Empty-outline onboarding suggests creating first 3 beats

## Non-goals
- AI-generated outlines
- Full timeline/dependency graph
- Community sharing of outlines

## Acceptance criteria
- User can create/edit outline fields per scene and data persists
- Dragging cards updates scene order reflected in chapter/scene navigator
- Card click deep-links into correct scene editor
- Status tags can be filtered (e.g., Drafted, Needs work, Final)
- Empty state includes clear call-to-action and no dead-end screen

## Priority rationale
This feature supports Living Writer-style structured planning and keeps writers inside one workflow. It strengthens differentiation from plain editors while remaining practical for MVP sequencing after core CRUD/autosave.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Create shareable read-only chapter preview links for private feedback
description:
## Problem
Writers need lightweight external feedback before publishing, but the app currently lacks a safe, low-friction way to share draft chapters without granting full edit access.

## Target user
Solo writers seeking feedback from trusted readers/critique partners during drafting and revision.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase number of stories that receive at least one external feedback cycle
- Reduce time from draft completion to first feedback received
- Improve retention of writers in revision stages

## Scope
- Add per-chapter `Share preview` action that creates a read-only link
- Allow writer to set optional expiration date and regenerate/revoke links
- Preview page shows chapter content only (no editor controls, no internal workspace)
- Capture lightweight reader reactions (thumbs up/down + short comment)
- Surface feedback entries back in writer’s chapter sidebar

## Non-goals
- Full public publishing/discovery feed
- Real-time collaborative editing permissions
- Advanced moderation/community management systems

## Acceptance criteria
- Writer can generate, copy, and revoke a preview link per chapter
- Link opens a read-only page accessible without app account
- Expired/revoked links no longer expose chapter content
- Reader feedback submitted through preview appears in writer view within the chapter context
- Sharing controls are clearly scoped to chapter-level content only

## Priority rationale
This introduces an Inkitt-inspired feedback loop in a controlled, private form that fits MVP progression and helps writers iterate faster without expanding into full community infrastructure.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Ship draft milestone planner with chapter-level progress and finish-date forecast
description:
## Problem
Many writers start projects but fail to finish because progress is not translated into a concrete manuscript completion path. Existing writing views show content, not a clear trajectory to “The End.”

## Target user
Writers in active first-draft phase who need lightweight accountability and visible momentum.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase percentage of active stories reaching “draft complete” status
- Improve week-over-week writing consistency for users who enable milestones
- Reduce abandonment rate after first 2 weeks of use

## Scope
- Add optional draft milestone setup: target draft length, target completion date, weekly writing cadence
- Compute chapter-level progress against draft plan and show remaining effort
- Surface a forecast indicator (on-track / at-risk) based on recent writing velocity
- Add dashboard entry in story workspace with actionable next milestone
- Allow users to pause, edit, or disable milestones without affecting manuscript data

## Non-goals
- Public accountability/community leaderboards
- Punitive streak mechanics or gamification-heavy UX
- AI-generated writing content to meet goals

## Acceptance criteria
- User can configure a milestone plan in under 2 minutes
- System shows current progress, next milestone, and forecast state for active story
- Forecast updates after writing sessions without manual refresh
- User can edit or disable milestone plan at any time
- Milestone data persists per story across reloads

## Priority rationale
Onboarding gets users started, but completion mechanics keep them through the full manuscript lifecycle. Shipping this now strengthens retention and aligns with user need to finish stories, not just organize them.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Ship manuscript export to DOCX and PDF with chapter-aware formatting
description:
## Problem
Writers need to share or submit manuscripts outside the app, but there is no dependable export path that preserves chapter structure and readable formatting. This creates lock-in anxiety and blocks practical adoption.

## Target user
Drafting and revising writers who need to send manuscripts to beta readers, editors, or submission channels in standard document formats.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase % of active stories that reach an export event
- Reduce churn risk signals related to data portability concerns
- Improve user satisfaction for end-to-end drafting workflow

## Scope
- Add `Export Manuscript` action at story level
- Support exports to `.docx` and `.pdf`
- Export includes title page (optional), chapter headings, scene breaks, and body text in reading order
- Provide basic formatting presets: manuscript standard and compact reading copy
- Include export confirmation with file type and timestamp

## Non-goals
- Query-package generation (synopsis, pitch letters, submission bundles)
- Advanced style customization controls
- Collaborative annotation inside exported documents

## Acceptance criteria
- User can export any story to DOCX and PDF without app crash or data loss
- Chapter/scene ordering in export matches in-app structure
- Formatting preset choice is applied correctly in output documents
- Exported files are downloadable locally with deterministic filenames
- Failed exports surface actionable error messaging and allow retry

## Priority rationale
Data portability is table-stakes for serious writers and directly supports trust in a writer-first product. A scoped, reliable export feature closes a core workflow gap without overreaching into publishing-suite complexity.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research

title: Reframe as story-aware focus sessions tied to scene objectives
description:
## Problem
Generic writing timers are now category-standard and do not reliably improve story completion quality on their own. Writers need focused sessions that connect directly to scene-level narrative outcomes, not just elapsed time and word counts.

## Target user
Fiction writers who draft in scenes and want structured sessions that help them finish specific narrative objectives (new beat, revision pass, dialogue polish) with less context-switching.

## Outcome / KPI
- Increase % of sessions with completed scene objective
- Increase multi-session continuity rate (users resume the suggested next scene within 24h)
- Improve week-2 retention among users who run story-aware focus sessions

## Scope
- Add `Story-aware Focus Session` mode in the editor with optional timer presets (15/25/45 minutes)
- Require selecting a target scene and objective type before starting (e.g., draft beat, revise pacing, tighten dialogue)
- Show live progress with both words-added and objective status (in progress / complete)
- End session with recap: words added, objective completion, and suggested next-scene handoff note
- Persist recent session history per story, including objective metadata

## Non-goals
- Social leaderboards, streak competitions, or multiplayer battles
- Advanced coaching/critique generated by AI
- Cross-story analytics dashboards in v1

## Acceptance criteria
- User can start/pause/end a focus session tied to a selected scene without data loss
- Objective selection is required at session start and stored in session history
- Session recap includes objective completion status and a next-step handoff note
- Save-state visibility remains present during active session mode
- Session settings/history persist across reload for the same user and story

## Priority rationale
Competitors already offer timer-only sprint tools; reframing around scene-objective completion creates a clearer story-first advantage and aligns with our goal to ship a writer workflow optimized for drafting outcomes.

## Handoff
Assign to: Market Research
